description: Cross-paper edges of the prime-number-theorem DAG. Direction reads subject-verb-object: '{from} {type} {to}'. Two groups: (A) intra-DAG edges between PNT papers; (B) cross-DAG edges to large-values-zero-density and maynard-small-gaps, whose target nodes live in those DAGs' papers/ files (the keystone links that join the analytic-number-theory region).

edges.note: Mossinghoff–Trudgian–Yang sharpen Ford's explicit Vinogradov–Korobov zero-free constant from $57.54$ to $55.241$.
The explicit classical-type zero-free constant improves Ford's $8.464$ to $5.558691$.
The asymptotic VK constant improves Ford's $0.05507$ to $0.05617776$.
MTY's zero-detector is stated as a direct application of Ford's Lemma 2.2 (the cotangent-integral identity).
MTY's degree-$K$ trigonometric positivity inequality generalizes Ford's Lemma 5.1.
MTY's medium-range engine follows Ford's Lemma 9.2 with a better $J(t)$, trig polynomial and effective height.
MTY's classical zero-free constant $5.558691$ sharpens the $R=5.573412$ region Platt–Trudgian used as input to their PNT error term.
Matomäki–Radziwiłł II extends the real-valued short-interval theorem to complex multiplicative functions with the sharp range $\rho<1/3-2/(3\pi)$ and an explicit pretentious main term.
The Parseval bound for sparse Dirichlet polynomials extends the main proposition of MR I to the complex / sparse setting.
Sharper count of short intervals lacking smooth numbers, improving the smooth-number corollary of MR I.

Both establish equidistribution of primes in APs to moduli near $x^{1/2}$ beyond Bombieri–Vinogradov — Shao–Teräväinen via nilsequence dispersion, Maynard III via uniform-residue Type II estimates — by independent methods.
CROSS-DAG → maynard-small-gaps. Maynard III's equidistributed minorant supplies the level-of-distribution beyond $1/2$ that the Maynard–Tao multidimensional sieve (Prop 4.1) consumes.
CROSS-DAG → maynard-small-gaps. Maynard III (uniform residue classes) is the sequel to Maynard I (fixed residue classes); both establish equidistribution of primes in APs to large moduli.

CROSS-DAG → maynard-small-gaps. Maynard III (uniform residue classes) and Maynard II (well-factorable weights at level $x^{3/5-\varepsilon}$) are companion papers in the large-moduli trilogy — III handles uniformity over residue classes, II handles the well-factorable / sieve-ready form that BFI-type sieves consume.
CROSS-DAG → maynard-small-gaps. Bounded gaps between primes in a nil-Bohr set extends the Maynard bounded-gaps theorem to primes constrained by $\|Q(n)\|<\rho$.

CROSS-DAG → large-values-zero-density. Platt–Trudgian's Pintz-type zero-density estimate $N(\sigma,T)\ll T^{8(1-\sigma)/3}\log^{5-2\sigma}T$ and Starichkova's classical zero-density route are the same Ingham–Pintz mechanism: a zero-density bound controlling primes in intervals.

CROSS-DAG → large-values-zero-density. The explicit zero-FREE region (no zeros near Re$(s)=1$) and the Guth–Maynard zero-DENSITY estimate $N(\sigma,T)\ll T^{15(1-\sigma)/(3+5\sigma)}$ are the two complementary controls on $\zeta$-zeros that bound prime distribution.
CROSS-DAG → large-values-zero-density. Ford's Vinogradov–Korobov zero-free region sharpens the qualitative zero-free region in Ingham's classical theorem (as recorded in the large-values DAG).
